coredata.dc:title: Flower-like FeOOH hybridized with carbon quantum dots for efficient photo-Fenton degradation of organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Pengfei
Zhou, Changli
Li, Yanpeng
Zhang, Minghui
Tao, Pengxian
Liu, Qingling
Cui, Wenquan
coredata.dc:description:
In this work, novel materials comprising three-dimensional flower-like αFeOOH with abundant active edges hybridized with carbon quantum dots (CQD@FeOOH) were fabricated via a facile and environmentally friendly two-step process including an electrochemical etching method followed by a urea/ethanol solution cooperating-mediated method. CQD@FeOOH exhibited remarkable degradation performance toward refractory phenol in a synergic photocatalytic/Fenton system, with a rate constant (k) 2.8 times higher than that of pristine αFeOOH. Moreover, the k value of CQD@FeOOH as a heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalyst was 12.6 and 3.1 times than those of CQD@FeOOH as photocatalysis and Fenton catalysis, respectively. The hybrid materials were characterized using scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, energy-dispersive spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy techniques to prove that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully modified on αFeOOH to form FeOC bonds. The electrochemical impedance spectra, transient photocurrent performance, photoluminescence spectra, and other photoelectric performance analyses confirmed the strong interaction between αFeOOH and the CQDs, which facilitated the rapid interfacial transfer of photogenerated electrons from αFeOOH to CQDs and enhanced the absorption of photons under visible light irradiation. Radical quenching experiments and electron spin resonance analysis demonstrated that
OH and
O2 − are the main active species for the synergic photo-Fenton degradation. Furthermore, CQD@FeOOH maintained high catalytic activity, high crystallinity, and extremely low iron leaching during repeated experiments. By optimizing degradation conditions, it was found that a low concentration of H2O2 and a wide pH range could maintain high catalytic performance. This work presents a feasible strategy to design and fabricate heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalysts for the removal of organic pollutants.

head.ce:title: Flower-like FeOOH hybridized with carbon quantum dots for efficient photo-Fenton degradation of organic pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this work, novel materials comprising three-dimensional flower-like αFeOOH with abundant active edges hybridized with carbon quantum dots (CQD@FeOOH) were fabricated via a facile and environmentally friendly two-step process including an electrochemical etching method followed by a urea/ethanol solution cooperating-mediated method. CQD@FeOOH exhibited remarkable degradation performance toward refractory phenol in a synergic photocatalytic/Fenton system, with a rate constant (k) 2.8 times higher than that of pristine αFeOOH. Moreover, the k value of CQD@FeOOH as a heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalyst was 12.6 and 3.1 times than those of CQD@FeOOH as photocatalysis and Fenton catalysis, respectively. The hybrid materials were characterized using scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, energy-dispersive spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy techniques to prove that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully modified on αFeOOH to form FeOC bonds. The electrochemical impedance spectra, transient photocurrent performance, photoluminescence spectra, and other photoelectric performance analyses confirmed the strong interaction between αFeOOH and the CQDs, which facilitated the rapid interfacial transfer of photogenerated electrons from αFeOOH to CQDs and enhanced the absorption of photons under visible light irradiation. Radical quenching experiments and electron spin resonance analysis demonstrated that
OH and
O2 − are the main active species for the synergic photo-Fenton degradation. Furthermore, CQD@FeOOH maintained high catalytic activity, high crystallinity, and extremely low iron leaching during repeated experiments. By optimizing degradation conditions, it was found that a low concentration of H2O2 and a wide pH range could maintain high catalytic performance. This work presents a feasible strategy to design and fabricate heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalysts for the removal of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para:
Industrial manufacturing processes, especially those that are related to the pharmaceutical, coal chemical industry, paper-making, printing, and dyeing industries, discharge organic pollutants that are highly toxic and difficult to degrade, seriously affecting water quality [1–3]. Moreover, some organic pollutants persist in water after treatment by traditional biochemical methods, and cannot be effectively removed by physical methods, such as air flotation, coagulation, and membrane separation. Therefore, in recent years, the development of effective, cost-efficient, stable, and safe water treatment technologies has attracted considerable attention from researchers. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are technologies for treating stubborn wastewater by adopting physical methods, such as sound, light, electricity and magnetism, or chemical methods to generate active hydroxyl radicals (
OH) exhibiting strong oxidizability (E0(H2O/
OH) = 2.8 V vs. NHE) [4], such as Fenton oxidation technology, photocatalytic oxidation technology, ozone oxidation technology, electrocatalytic oxidation technology, and ultrasonic oxidation technology [5–9]. The Fenton reaction employs a mixture of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and ferrous iron (Fe2+) in an acidic solution to generate
OH, which is then used to degrade organic compounds in wastewater into small molecular organics or mineralize them into CO2 and H2O. However, the Fenton reaction has several limitations. For instance, the production of iron residue leads to unwanted Fe2+ consumption and secondary pollution, and its the high degradation activity is limited to acidic conditions [10]. To overcome these problems, heterogeneous Fenton-like systems that supports iron-based catalyst on materials such as on porous activated carbon, graphene, mesoporous silicon, and zeolites have been proposed [6,11–13]. Because heterogeneous catalysis is an interface reaction, the number of surface active sites influences the activity of a catalyst. The low efficiency of the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle also causes a high consumption of H2O2. To further improve the cycle efficiency of Fe3+/Fe2+ and reduce the consumption of H2O2, technologies combining photocatalysis and Fenton to treat organic pollutants have emerged [14]. Currently, research in this field is focused on the development of suitable catalytic materials, with excellent degradation performance for organic pollutants, a wide pH operating range, and high catalytic stability.

For semiconductor catalytic materials, forbidden band widths, photogenerated electron-hole pairs excitation, separation, transfer efficiency, surface reaction rate, H2O2 decomposition rate, Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle rate, surface adsorption capacity, and number of active sites influence their photo-Fenton degradation performance. In recent years, studies on the degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater using various iron-based materials under different light sources and involving H2O2 have been reported [15–19]. Among them, goethite (αFeOOH) is widely used in biosensors, lithium ion batteries, water restoration, electrode materials, and semiconductor materials, because it is highly available in soil, rocks, and water on the Earth’s surface [20]. As a transition metal oxide semiconductor, αFeOOH has a narrow band gap of about 2.6 eV and exhibits high absorptive ability in light of the visible region, but charge-carrier recombination lowers its efficiency. Accordingly, the strategies currently available to improve the photocatalytic performance of αFeOOH are as follows: (i) Doping or adjusting the shape of the material to change its surface properties, thereby adjusting the width of the band gap and expanding the range of light response [21–23]. (ii) Adding cocatalysts, such as mesoporous activated carbon and introducing oxygen vacancies as the active sites of the catalytic reaction, which accelerates the transport of electrons between the material interfaces and improves charge-carrier separation efficiency [10,24,25]. (iii) Hybridizing with other semiconductor materials, such as Fe2O3, BiVO4, and C3N4, to form heterojunctions and built-in electric fields, which changes the positions of the conduction and valence bands [23,26,27]. However, if the semiconductor composite is large or the dispersion is uneven, then the contact interfaces become insufficient and incompact. This will lower conductivity and can form new recombination centers, resulting in decreased catalytic performance. The researchers found that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) materials can be uniformly dispersed in a base material and form close contact to promote the rapid conduction of carriers.

CQDs are a class of sub-10-nm carbon-based materials composed of carbon atoms in sp2/sp3 hybridization and connected with oxygen-containing functional groups. The sp2 hybrids form single-layer or multilayer graphene quantum dots, and the sp3 hybrids form spherical carbon quantum dots with obvious lattice structure [28]. The π-conjugated structure of CQDs can serve as an electron acceptor and donor and contribute to the transfer of photogenerated electrons. Up-conversion by CQDs can capture and utilize light in a wider range of wavelengths [29]. In recent years, researchers have used CQDs to modify various semiconductor catalysts, such as Fe2O3, Fe3O4, NiFe(OH)2, Bi2O2CO3, Bi2WO6 [30–34].

In the present study, we uniformly dispersed CQDs on the iron-based catalyst αFeOOH. Under the synergistic effects of light irradiation and H2O2, the hybrid catalyst showed better organic pollutant degradation performance than that of pristine αFeOOH. Furthermore, a transfer path for photogenerated electrons in the presence of CQDs was proposed. The mechanism of heterogeneous photo-Fenton degradation of pollutants in this system was discussed in detail based on electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectral analysis, and free radical trapping experiments. Thus, this work provides a new strategy to promote photo-Fenton reactions.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. The preparation of high-purity CQDs was performed by an electrochemical etching method that is facile, economical, and environmentally friendly [35]. The preparation process and resulting product are illustrated in Scheme 1 . In a typical procedure, two high-purity graphite rod electrodes (99.99%; L: 100 mm; Φ: 6 mm; Jinglong Carbon Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing) were immersed in 300 mL ultrapure water (18.5 MΩ cm−1) with continuous stirring. The distance between the two electrodes was 50 mm. Then a static potential of 40 V was applied to the two electrodes using a direct current power supply. Over 96 h of continuous electrolysis, the anode graphite rod was gradually oxidized and etched, and the color of the electrolyte changed from light-yellow to dark-brown. The electrolyte was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 30 min) to remove the precipitated graphite particles, and then filtered with an aqueous phase filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Finally, a high-purity CQD dispersion with a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL was obtained.

By the method of urea/ethanol solution cooperating-mediated, the concentration of ethanol solution was used to control the crystal growth rate as a means to self-assemble three-dimensional flower-like CQD@FeOOH. The preparation procedure for CQD@FeOOH is shown in Scheme 2 . In a typical procedure, 0.02 mol FeSO4·7H2O and 0.02 mol urea were completely dissolved in 200 mL aqueous ethanol (alc. 20% vol.). A certain volume of the CQD dispersion was added and the mixture was ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min. Then, the above solution was transferred to a round-bottom flask and refluxed in a water bath at 80 °C under air atmosphere for 5 h with continuous stirring. Subsequently, the flask was cooled to room temperature naturally. The product was separated by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and water several times, dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 5 h, and ground to obtain a yellow–brown powder.

Pure αFeOOH was synthesized without adding CQDs under the same conditions. According to the volume of CQD dispersion added to the αFeOOH solution, the products were labeled αFeOOH, 5CQD@FeOOH, 10CQD@FeOOH, 20CQD@FeOOH, or 30CQD@FeOOH.

The crystal structures and phase states of the samples were investigated by automatic powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) using a X'Pert PRO MPD (PANalytical, Netherlands, set at 40 kV and 150 mA, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.154 nm) and recorded over the range 10° < 2θ < 80° with a scan rate of 10°/min. Particle morphology and size were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, GeminiSEM 300, ZEISS, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2800F, JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was used to analyze the distribution of elements on the CQD@FeOOH surface. X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) was used to determine the chemical status of the samples (EscaLab Xi+, Thermo Fisher, US, set at 14.4 kV and 13.6 mA, passing-energy 100 eV/30 eV, five-cycle signal accumulation). The functional groups of the samples were identified by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, VERTEX70, BRUKER, Germany). Raman spectra were obtained with a Renishaw inVia plus (Renishaw, UK) with a 532 nm laser as excitation source. The surface area and pore size distribution were measured by nitrogen adsorption with an Autosorb IQ specific surface area analyzer (Quantachrome Instrument Corp., USA) using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Pore size was calculated using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) technique. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra were also recorded (UV–Vis DRS, UV-9000S, METASH, China) with BaSO4 as the reference material. PL spectra were acquired at room temperature using a F-7000 luminescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The ESR signals of radicals spin-trapped by the spin-capture reagent 5,5′-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) in water and methanol were examined on an A300 BRUKER system. Transient photocurrent and impedance tests were performed in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution in a three-electrode quartz cell with a 350 W mercury lamp and a 320 nm cut-off filter using a CHI660B electrochemical workstation. αFeOOH or the CQD@FeOOH hybrid was used as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode, and a Pt wire was used as the counter electrode. Iron ion leaching rates in organic pollutant solutions were obtained by an atomic absorption TAS-990 spectrophotometer (Puxi, China).

The photo-Fenton degradation of organic pollutants was conducted in a multi-tube photochemical instrument (XPA-7, Xujiang China), which was connected to a cooling device to keep the reaction solution temperature constant at 30 ± 2 °C. A 350 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used to simulate visible light, and a 350 W mercury lamp with a 320 nm cut-off filter was used to simulate the ultraviolet light. Toxic and refractory phenol was employed as a model pollutant for degradation, with a distance of 5 cm between the light source and the reaction system. A certain amount of catalyst was used to degrade a certain concentration of 30 mL phenol solution. The reaction solution was stirred for 30 min in the darkness to allow the adsorption equilibrium. Then, H2O2 was added and the lamp was turned on. A 1 mL sample of the reaction suspension was collected every 10 min. The sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Chromaster5430 HITACHI Japan). The HPLC column was a 15 cm × 4.6 mm C18 column with a particle size of 5 μm. The mobile phase consisted of methanol/water (60:40, v/v). The flow rate was 1 mL/min and column temperature of 25 °C.

The photo-Fenton mineralization rate of organic pollutant solutions was detected with a total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (TOC-L, Shimadzu, Japan). A 15 mL sample was centrifuged to remove the catalyst.

The degradation and mineralization degrees of the samples were evaluated by HPLC and TOC respectively. Degradation efficiency (DEt) is defined as:
(1)

C 0

where C 0 is the initial concentration and C t is the time-dependent concentration of the organics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and structural properties of CQD@FeOOH
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphological characteristics of pure αFeOOH were investigated by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (a, b), a number of self-assembled three-dimensional flower-like microspheres with a diameter of ~1 μm are observed. Each microsphere has a large number of nanorods radiating from the center. Each nanorod is ~500 nm long and 30 nm wide. The morphology of the catalytic material is closely related to the amount of H2O2 adsorbed, which directly affects the formation of
OH in the Fenton reaction [36]. Fig. 1(c, d) shows TEM images of CQD@FeOOH. The morphology of in-situ synthesized CQD@FeOOH is not significantly different to that of pure αFeOOH, demonstrating that the addition of CQDs does not affect the formation of nanorods and flower-like microspheres. The nanorods are thin, sharp, and uniformly dispersed, which is beneficial to the photocatalytic activity of the material [37]. This morphology may be due to the formation of Fe(II)-ethanol complexes with Fe(II) coordinated to ethanol molecules in the solution, and these complexes aggregating to form a quasi-emulsion system that acts as a nucleation center. Under the weakly alkaline environment provided by the urea solution at 80 °C, Fe(II) is continuously adsorbed, and the αFeOOH crystals grow continuously to form nano- or sheet-like structures [38].

To confirm that the CQDs were successfully combined with αFeOOH to form CQD@FeOOH hybrid materials, HRTEM, EDS, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were performed (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2(a) shows an HRTEM image of the prepared αFeOOH in which the large-area lattice fringe with a lattice spacing of 0.247 nm is observed, corresponding to the (1 1 1) crystal plane of αFeOOH [39]. Fig. 2(b) shows an HRTEM image of CQD@FeOOH. CQDs with a size of 2–4 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.321 nm (corresponding to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of graphite carbon) are evenly anchored on the surface of αFeOOH, indicating that the CQDs are successfully hybridized with the αFeOOH [34]. Fig. 2(c) shows the SAED pattern of αFeOOH, which indicates high crystallinity, and this result is consistent with those of HRTEM. The elemental composition of CQD@FeOOH was obtained using EDS analysis. The EDS spectrum shown in Fig. 2(d) reveals that the CQD@FeOOH hybrid materials contain only Fe, O, and C, which confirms the existence of CQDs on the surface of αFeOOH.

To further establish that CQDs were successfully introduced onto αFeOOH to form a CQD@FeOOH hybrid material, XRD and FT-IR analysis were performed (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3(a) shows the XRD patterns of CQDs, αFeOOH and CQD@FeOOH. The main diffraction peaks of pure αFeOOH are located at 17.79°, 21.22°, 26.32°, 33.24°, 34.70°, 36.65°, 39.98°, 41.19°, and 53.24°, corresponding to the (0 2 0), (1 1 0), (1 2 0), (1 3 0), (0 2 1), (1 1 1), (1 2 1), (1 4 0), and (2 2 1) crystal planes, respectively, which is consistent with the conclusion reported in the literature [40,41]. All the diffraction peaks can be matched with pure orthorhombic iron oxyhydroxide (JCPDS card no. 29-0713). Furthermore, comparing with the Fe2O3 standard diffraction spectra (JCPDS No. 33-0664), it is found that no characteristic peaks match the synthesized sample, indicating that there is no Fe2O3 in the synthesized sample (Fig. S1) [30,42]. No impurity diffraction peaks are observed. The strong diffraction peak at 26.38° for corresponds to the graphite phase (sp2 hybrid CC) crystal plane (0 0 2), which is consistent with the graphite standard card (JCPDS card no. 41-1487) [35]. Due to the low content of CQDs in the CQD@FeOOH hybrid material, its characteristic diffraction peaks could not be observed. However, with increasing CQDs content, the characteristic diffraction peak intensities gradually decrease and the full width at half maxima (FWHM) values increase, indicating the effect of CQDs on the crystallinity of the hybrid materials.

FT-IR spectra were collected to investigate the interaction between the CQDs and αFeOOH (Fig. 3(b)). The strong flexural vibration peaks at 888 and 790 cm−1 for αFeOOH and CQD@FeOOH are caused by the in-plane bending of hydroxyl groups on the surface of Fe-OH [43]. The stretching vibration peak near 630 cm−1 is attributed to FeO in the αFeOOH structure [44]. For pure CQDs, the absorption peaks at 1220, 1430, and 1708 cm−1 correspond to COH, CC (sp2 hybrids), and CO in the carboxyl groups respectively, indicating that the precursor anode graphite rod was oxidized[32]. The intensity of these characteristic peaks for CQD@FeOOH are lower than that for pristine αFeOOH, which is caused by the substitution of the surface hydroxyl function groups with COH of CQDs. In the FT-IR spectra of CQD@FeOOH, new bending vibration peak at 1028 cm−1 near the characteristic peaks of CO is appeared, which prove the existence of FeOC bond [10,45]. Comparing with pristine αFeOOH, the slight shift in the FeO absorption bands indicates the existence of strong electrostatic interactions between the CQDs and αFeOOH, which further demonstrates that the FeOC bonds are formed successfully, and this is in good agreement with the related literature [6,46,47]. Due to the low content of CQDs, their absorption is not detected in the spectrum of CQD@FeOOH, which is consistent with the XRD results.

The existence of CQDs in the CQD@FeOOH composite is further verified by Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3(c). CQDs show the existence of two Raman peaks at 1348.5 cm−1 and 1590.4 cm−1, which can be indexed to D-band (disordered sp2 carbon) and G-band (conjugated sp2 carbon), respectively [48]. In the Raman spectrum of CQD@FeOOH composite, the shifting of both D and G bands of CQDs suggests strong interaction and well incorporation of iron hydroxide cations on the negative surface of CQDs. Compared with CQDs, the increase in the intensity ratio of D bands and G bands (ID/IG) of which confirms decrease of the sp2 domain of carbon [20].

The textural structure of synthesized flower-like CQD@FeOOH nanostructures can be determined from the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm presented in Fig. 3(d). The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm shows a typical type IV curve with type H3 hysteresis loop within the 0.6–1.0 range of relative pressure according to the IUPAC classification, which implies an irregular mesoporous structure formed by aggregation particles[1]. The specific surface area of CQD@FeOOH is 150.66 m2/g. The pore-size distribution shown in the insert of Fig. 3(d) reveals that the catalyst contains a large number of mesopores (3–50 nm) with two peaks at pore diameters of ~3.4 and 18.1 nm as well as a small amount of micropores (<2 nm) and macropores (>50 nm). The average pore width is 12.45 nm and the pore volume is 0.407 cm3/g. These results demonstrate the existence of hierarchically nano-porous structures in the prepared samples on multiple length scales.

Toxic and refractory phenol was used as the target degradation product, and equal amounts of catalyst were used to degrade phenol in solutions at a concentration of 5 ppm. The phenol degradation activities of αFeOOH and CQD@FeOOH under Fenton, photocatalysis, and photo-Fenton conditions were compared. From Fig. 4 (a), it can be seen that the synergistic photo-Fenton degradation activity of αFeOOH alone is significantly better than those of its Fenton reaction and the photocatalytic reaction activities individually. However, when successfully modified with CQDs, the catalytic activities under Fenton, photocatalysis, and photo-Fenton conditions are improved. The degradation efficiency for 50 min under photo-Fenton conditions is 100%, while the degradation efficiencies for photocatalysis and Fenton conditions are 31.4% and 80.4%, respectively (Fig. 4(b)). At the same time, the degradation performance of CQDs toward phenol was investigated. Fig. S2 in supplementary material shows that the introduction of ultraviolet light irradiation and hydrogen peroxide have no obvious improvement on the phenol degradation.

Using quasi-first-order kinetics to calculate the degradation rate constant for phenol under different conditions, the calculation formula is as follows [49]:
(2)
I n (
C 0
/ C ) = k t
where k is the apparent rate constant, which is an important parameter reflecting the degradation rate for the catalytic process, t is the reaction time, C is the concentration of phenol at time t, and C 0 is the initial concentration of phenol.

Fig. 4(c) and (d) show a linear fitting graph of ln(C 0/C) plotted against t of αFeOOH and CQD@FeOOH, where the slope of the fitted line is the apparent rate constant k, as shown in the inset. The k of CQD@FeOOH for the photo-Fenton degradation is 3.1 times and 12.6 times those of Fenton catalysis and photocatalysis, respectively, indicating that H2O2 plays an important role in phenol degradation. Other kinetic parameters are listed in Table S1.

When αFeOOH is modified with CQDs, the catalytic activities for photocatalysis, Fenton and photo-Fenton conditions are improved. There are three possible reasons for this, as follows: First, CQDs have a π-π conjugated structure that can act as an electron acceptor and, when hybridized with αFeOOH, form FeOC coordination bonds that quickly transfer photogenerated electrons from αFeOOH to the CQDs. Thus, the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and photocatalytic activity are improved. Second, CQDs contain a large number of oxygen-containing hydrophilic functional groups, which can adsorb more H2O and H2O2 and participate in photocatalysis and the Fenton reaction. Third, in addition to some of the photogenerated electrons promoting the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle, some of the photogenerated electrons directly react with H2O2 adsorbed on the surface of the CQDs to generate
OH, providing Fenton-like activity, which further improves the photocatalytic phenol degradation performance.

Fig. 4(e) shows a 2D HPLC trace for photo-Fenton degradation of phenol over CQD@FeOOH, in which the retention time of the phenol peak is ~5.17 min. As the reaction progresses, the intensity of the phenol peak decreases, and no phenol signal is detected after 50 min, indicating that the phenol is completely degraded.

In order to determine why CQD@FeOOH significantly improves phenol degradation under the combined action of light and H2O2, the chemical compositions and chemical states of the elements on the surfaces of αFeOOH and CQD@FeOOH were analyzed using XPS, and the results are given in Fig. 5 . The survey spectrums in Fig. 5(a) and (b) show signals for Fe 2p, O 1s, C 1s and S 2p. The presence of S is due to the precursor FeSO4. The presence of C in αFeOOH survey spectrum is due to the residual carbon from the sample and adventitious hydrocarbon from the XPS instrument itself [50]. The high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectrum of pure αFeOOH shows a binding energy peak at 711.5 eV (Fe 2p 3/2) along with a satellite peak at 719.4 eV and a peak at 725.3 eV (Fe 2p 1/2) together with a satellite peak at 732.9 eV, of which the Fe 2p 3/2 peak can be deconvoluted into two peaks (711.3 eV and 713.8 eV) corresponding to FeOOH and ferric iron salts, respectively, which is consistent with the literature (Fig. 5(c)) [51,52]. The binding energy positions for CQD@FeOOH are positively shifted by 0.3 eV, and the two main peaks are located at 711.8 and 725.6 eV, indicating an electronic interaction between the CQDs and αFeOOH. There are two chemical states for O 1s, namely FeOFe bonds (530.0 eV) and FeOH (531.4 eV) (Fig. 5(d)) [53]. Upon to the addition of CQDs, the O 1s binding energy of CQD@FeOOH is positively shifted, confirms that the surface center metal electrons on the αFeOOH were transferred to CQDs as electrons acceptor, which also proved the formation of FeOC bonds [6,45]. This conclusion is consistent with the results of FT-IR analysis. The existence of FeOC bonds decreases electron-transfer resistance and inhibits recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs to improved photocatalytic activity [10].

In order to investigate the optical response and charge migration in the materials, their photoelectronic performances of the materials were evaluated. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CQD dispersion shows strong absorption of ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light, and the characteristic peak is located at 326 nm (Fig. S3). Being a photosensitizer, CQDs have a wide spectral absorption range, which is beneficial to the optical quantum efficiency of a photocatalytic composite material. The light absorption characteristics of αFeOOH and CQD@FeOOH hybrid materials with different addition ratios were compared (Fig. 6 (a)). The light-harvesting capabilities of CQD@FeOOH hybrid materials in the ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared regions are improved, and the light response range is expanded. Increasing light absorption capacity stimulates the formation of more charge carriers, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity of the material. With increasing CQDs addition, the color of CQD@FeOOH changes from brownish-yellow to dark-brown (inset, Fig. 6(a)). The absorption spectrum of αFeOOH is divided into three regions. The 200–400 nm region shows ligand-to-metal charge-transfer (L-M CT); 400–600 nm region contains signals for the L-M CT tail and the d-d transition of the metal itself; and the 600–750 nm region shows mainly metal d-d transition. For pure αFeOOH, the excitation ligand-to-metal electronic transitions O → Fe and OH → Fe are mainly generated by ultraviolet light, while visible light only stimulates the d-d transition of the metal itself, which is consistent with the relevant literature [54]. Upon modification with CQDs, the L-M CT electronic transition is extended to the visible light. By comparing the phenol degradation activities obtained under ultraviolet and visible light, visible light can also promote Fenton activity, which is consistent with the conclusion of UV–Vis DRS. However, under the synergistic effect of visible light and H2O2, the degradation activity of CQD@FeOOH is not significantly higher than that under ultraviolet light. This may be due to the small amount of CQDs, as shown in Fig. S4.

The band gap energy of obtained CQD@FeOOH hybrid materials were calculated using the transformed Kubelka–Munk function (Eq. (3)) shown in Fig. 6(b).
(3)
α h υ = A
( h υ -
E g
)
n
where α, υ, A, and E g are the absorption coefficient, light frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap energy, respectively, and n describes the type of the transition in a semiconductor, that is n = 1/2 for direct transition and n = 2 for indirect transition [20]. The value of n for CQD@FeOOH composites is taken as 1/2. The band gap energy of the photocatalyst is estimated from the plot of (αhυ)n vs. hυ by extrapolating the straight line to the X axis intercept, by which the roughly estimated band gap energy is 2.22 eV corresponding to CQD@FeOOH. The valence band (VB) of CQD@FeOOH is determined to be 1.31 eV vs. NHE shown inset of Fig. 6(b), the conduction band position (CB) was about −0.91 eV vs. NHE, which can be calculated by ECB = EVB − Eg. The produced electrons can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ (E0 (Fe3+/Fe2+) = 0.771 eV vs. NHE) increasing the cycling of iron species in Fenton reaction.

PL analysis was used to investigate the photogenerated charge-carrier migration and recombination process. Fig. 6(c) shows that the prepared materials produce PL spectra at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The lower fluorescence intensities for CQD@FeOOH indicates that the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is lower [3]. When CQDs are anchored to αFeOOH, the PL intensity is significantly reduced, which indicates that the photogenerated electrons migrate from the conduction band of αFeOOH to the CQDs, thereby decreasing electron-hole recombination. When H2O2 is added, the PL intensity further decreases, indicating that the photogenerated electrons are consumed in the Fenton reaction, meaning that photogenerated electron-hole recombination is further suppressed.

Fig. 6(d) shows the PL spectra of the CQD dispersion at different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength is in the range 250–500 nm, the emission peaks for the CQDs are red-shifted. This may be due to stacking and reorganization of the local electron-hole pairs of sp2 clusters in the graphite structure of the CQDs [35,55,56]. When the excitation wavelength is between 600 nm and 900 nm, the emission peaks are a blue-shifted. Furthermore, because CQDs are both excellent electron donors and acceptors, the spectral intensity is decreased [57].

In order to investigate the electron-hole pair separation efficiencies of the prepared materials, their transient photocurrent performances were investigated. Fig. 7 (a) shows the I-t curves of the materials when a mercury lamp was turned off and on repeatedly at fixed periods [19]. The photocurrent response of pure αFeOOH as the working electrode is significantly lower than that of CQD@FeOOH. The conjugated π-bond structure of the CQDs acts as an electron acceptor, allowing the photogenerated electrons of αFeOOH excited from the valence band to the conduction band to quickly transfer to the CQDs, improving the efficiency of charge separation. When a certain concentration of H2O2 is added to the electrolyte, the photocurrent intensity is reduced by 85%. Since some of the photogenerated electrons are captured by H2O2 to generate
OH, these consumed electrons do not pass through the external detection circuit [58].

In order to study the transfer of photogenerated electrons at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed (Fig. 7(b)). A smaller curvature radius in an EIS Nyquist plot indicates better electron-hole separation and a faster photocatalytic reaction [27]. Due to the addition of CQDs, the photogenerated electrons of the CQD@FeOOH hybrid materials are rapidly transferred, meaning that the interface charges can be transferred to the acceptor more quickly. When H2O2 is added, more photogenerated electrons are consumed and the separation of charges is promoted. The curvature radius of the Nyquist arc increases, which was consistent with the results of photocurrent analysis [14].

In order to propose a reasonable reaction mechanism for photo-Fenton degradation of phenol over CQD@FeOOH, quenching experiments were conducted to determine the main active species in the degradation process. Fig. 8 shows the degradation of phenol over 50 min with the addition of different quenchers. Isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), and Cr(VI) were used to quench
OH,
O2 −, h+, and e−, respectively. When IPA is added to the reaction system, the degradation process is significantly inhibited. After 50 min of reaction, the degradation efficiency is 18.5%, which indicates that
OH plays a major role in the photo-Fenton degradation of phenol. The addition of BQ also affects the degradation of phenol, and the degradation efficiency decreases to 67.3%.

In order to investigate the effect of dissolved O2 on the formation of
O2 −, N2, or O2 were bubbled into the reaction system. The amount of dissolved O2 decreases after purging with N2, inhibiting the formation of
O2 − on the catalyst surface (e− + O2 →
O2 −), which leads to a decrease in degradation efficiency. The introduction of O2 increases the amount of
O2 −, and the degradation efficiency of phenol increases, indicating that
O2 − is a minor active species. Potassium dichromate was used to quench photogenerated electrons because the reduction potential of Cr(VI) (E0 (Cr2O7 2−/Cr3+) = +1.232 eV vs. SCE) is higher than those of H2O2 (E0 (H2O2/
OH) = +0.38 eV vs. SCE) and O2 (E0 (O2/
O2 −) = − 0.33 eV vs. SCE), so e− reacts with Cr(VI) more readily[58]. In the present study, the addition of Cr(VI) was found to inhibit the formation of free radicals and decreased phenol degradation efficiency. The difference between degradation efficiency with and without EDTA-2Na is only 4.7%, indicating that h+ is not the main active species for phenol degradation.

The ESR spin-trap technique was used to further verify the presence of the main active species (
OH and
O2 −) in the photo-Fenton system (Fig. 9 ). Under dark conditions, DMPO-
O2 − produces no signal, and DMPO-
OH produces a weak characteristic peak because CQD@FeOOH reacts with H2O2 to form a Fenton reaction producing a small amount of
OH. After light irradiation, the obvious 1:2:2:1 four-wire signal characteristic peak corresponding DMPO-
OH is observed (Fig. 9(a)), and a strong 1:1:1:1 quadruple signal characteristic peak corresponding to DMPO-
O2 − with the extension of the irradiation time is observed in Fig. 9(b) [14,59]. Therefore,
OH and
O2 − free radicals are produced by CQD@FeOOH and H2O2 under illumination conditions, which is consistent with the quenching experiment conclusions.

In order to further determine the formation path of the main active species
OH, the relationship between
OH generation and H2O2 consumption was explored. The amounts of
OH formed in 20 min under different reaction conditions was explored using coumarin (COU) as a fluorescent probe. The excitation wavelength was set to 342 nm, and the fluorescence peak was located at 456 nm. The higher the
OH content, the stronger the fluorescence peak. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), when CQD@FeOOH and H2O2 are both present, a strong
OH fluorescence peak is produced under irradiation. Adding the same amount of H2O2 to the reaction system under dark condition also produces an
OH fluorescence peak, but its intensity is lower than that for photo-Fenton conditions. The reaction system presents a weak
OH fluorescence peak under irradiation without H2O2. These results further demonstrate that the photo-Fenton system accelerates
OH formation.

The interaction between H2O2 and TiOSO4/H2SO4 forms a yellow complex, so the content of H2O2 in the reaction system was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometry. Fig. 10(b) shows the decomposition of H2O2 at different reaction times under the action of CQD@FeOOH. As the reaction proceeds, the H2O2 content gradually decreases, indicating that under photo-Fenton conditions, H2O2 continuously decomposed to produce
OH. However, the degradation activity over CQD@FeOOH in the absence of H2O2 is significantly lower than that under photo-Fenton conditions, which indicates that the source of
OH is mainly the decomposition of H2O2 and that no
OH is produced under irradiation.

Based on the above experimental results and conclusions, a process for the degradation of phenol over CQD@FeOOH under the combined action of H2O2 and irradiation is proposed (Scheme 3 ). Hydroxyl radicals as the main active species for phenol degradation are produced in following two ways. First, under irradiation, the αFeOOH is excited to produce electron-hole pairs. Since CQDs are successfully applied on the αFeOOH to form FeOC bonds, electrons are quickly transferred between the Fe center and H2O2 [6]. As an electron acceptor, CQDs receives a large amount of photogenerated electrons and react with adsorbed H2O2 to form
OH, inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs and improving the efficiency of phenol degradation (Eqs. (4) and (5))[31]. Second, some of the photogenerated electrons are excited from the ligand to the metal (O → Fe, OH → Fe), reducing Fe3+ species to Fe2+ species [54]. The Fe2+ 3d orbital and the sp3 hybrid orbital of H2O2 adsorbed on the catalyst surface partially overlap, which weakens the OO bond [14]. This is conducive to the decomposition of H2O2 to generate a large amount of highly reactive
OH for phenol degradation. Due to the participation of photogenerated electrons, the consumption of H2O2 by the reduction of Fe3+ is decreased. The effective Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle ensures that enough Fe2+ participate in the photo-Fenton reaction (Eqs. (6) and (7)) [60,61]. At the same time, some of the photogenerated electrons react with dissolved O2 in the system to generate
O2 −, which participates in the degradation of phenol (Eq. (11)). Although holes do not oxidize phenol, they react with H2O2 to form O2 and H+, which provides more O2 to form
O2 − (Eq. (11)).
(4) CQD@FeOOH + hν → e− + h+
(5) H2O2 + e− →
OH + OH−
(6) e− + Fe3+ → Fe2+
(7) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ +
OH + OH−
(8) H2O2 + hν →
OH
(9) Fe3+ + H2O2 → Fe2+ +
O2H + H+
(10) Fe3+ +
O2H → Fe2+ + O2 + H+
(11) e− + O2 →
O2 −
(12) h+ + H2O2 → O2 + H+

The previous results indicate that the addition of CQDs to αFeOOH by in-situ synthesis significantly improves the efficiency of photo-Fenton phenol degradation. Next, the effect of different parameters on phenol degradation efficiency were explored. Fig. 11 (a) shows the degradation efficiencies at 20 and 50 mins (DE20 and DE50) for CQD@FeOOH with different CQD loadings. With the increase of CQD loading, DE20 increases from 90.1% to 92.3% and then decreases to 84.7% and 54.3%, while DE50 (except that for 30CQD@FeOOH) remains at 100% irrespective of CQD loading. 10CQD@FeOOH has the highest catalytic activity with k = 0.125 min−1. Therefore, the proper addition amount and high dispersion of CQDs favor degradation efficiency. There are three main reasons for this. (i) CQDs as electron acceptors in the photocatalytic process can quickly transfer the electrons excited by light in the metal active center, completing the separation of electron-hole pairs and improving photocatalytic efficiency; (ii) CQDs with higher electron density prefer to react with electrophilic H2O2 (E0 (H2O2/
OH) = 0.38 eV vs. SCE) rather than dissolving oxygen (E0 (O2/
O2 −) = -0.33 eV vs. SCE); (iii) Phenol and CQDs can form a π-π conjugate interaction, adsorbing phenol on the surface of CQD@FeOOH[62]. However, excessive amounts of CQDs cause a decrease in uniformity and lead to agglomeration, decreasing the number of active sites and potentially forming new recombination centers, leading to a decrease in photocatalytic efficiency.

Fig. 11(b) shows a comparison of the degradation efficiencies of different catalyst concentrations. As the catalyst concentration increases from 0.25 g/L to 1 g/L, DE20 increases from 68.6% to 92.3%. With further increase in catalyst concentration, DE20 gradually decreases. When the catalyst concentration is 0.5, 1, and 1.5 g/L, DE50 is 100%. When the concentration is increased to 2 g/L, phenol is not completely degraded within a 50 min reaction time. This demonstrates that as more catalyst is added to the system, more adsorption sites and active sites can be used to activate H2O2 to produce
OH, which increases the degradation rate. However, an excessive catalyst concentration will affect the light transmittance of the system and affect the capture of light by the catalyst. Although the degradation efficiency for 0.5 g/L catalyst dosage is slightly lower than that of the 1 g/L catalyst dosage at 20 min reaction, it completely degrades phenol at 50 min reaction. Thus, considering the cost of the catalyst, a catalyst concentration of 0.5 g/L is more economical.

Fig. 11(c) shows that when the H2O2 concentration in the system increases from 4 mM to 12 mM, the corresponding DE20 increases from 57.9% to 87.1%, which is because more H2O2 can react with Fe2+ to produce
OH. When the concentration of H2O2 increases to 16 mM, both DE20 and DE50 begin to decline and the phenol is not completely degraded after 50 min of reaction. This is due to the excessive addition of H2O2, which causes
OH to undergo a competitive reaction between the pollutants and H2O2. Excess H2O2 reacts with
OH to form O2 and H2O, which leads to the consumption of
OH and H2O2 (Eqs. (13) and (14))[17]. DE20 at 8 mM and 12 mM are not significantly different (k 8mM = 0.090 min−1, k 12mM = 0.091 min−1), and phenol is completely degraded after 50 min of reaction. From the perspective of operating cost, a H2O2 concentration of 8 mM is optimal.
(13)
OH + H2O2 →
OH2 + H2O
(14)
OH +
OH2 → O2 + H2O

Fig. 11(d) shows the effect of different initial pH values on photo-Fenton phenol degradation. In order to prevent the leaching of large amounts of metal ions, the pH was adjusted to >4. Therefore, the homogeneous Fenton reaction can be ignored, and the degradation of phenol mainly occurs on the surface of the catalyst. With an increase in pH, the concentration of iron ions in the solution drops from 0.41 mg/L to 0.014 mg/L. It was found that the catalyst has a high degradation efficiency under acidic or neutral conditions, with DE20 exceeding 80%, and phenol degrading completely within 50 min of reaction. When the initial pH increases from 9 to 11, DE50 decreases from 33.9% to 25.9%. As shown in Fig. S6, the pH value of point zero charge (pHpzc) for 10CQD@FeOOH is ~6.6. Due to the existence of electrostatic repulsion, peracid or peralkaline species are not conducive to the adsorption of pollutants on the catalyst surface. The catalytic activity decreases significantly in an alkaline condition, which is due to the large amount of OH− adsorbed on the catalyst surface replacing H2O2, the decomposition of some of the H2O2 into H2O and O2 affects the production of
OH [63]. Furthermore, under alkaline conditions, Fe3+ reacts with OH− to form a precipitate, which suppresses the circulation of Fe3+/Fe2+ and deposits on the surface of the catalyst result in a decrease of activity. The catalyst exhibits high catalytic activity in both acidic and neutral conditions, indicating that the materials have a wide pH adaptability range can achieve a higher degradation effect without acidification when treating phenol wastewater, which is very important for the application of the catalyst. It is well known that the cost of adjusting the pH during operation is higher than the energy consumed by light irradiation or the cost of adding H2O2 [60].

In order to further explore the general applicability of 10CQD@FeOOH, photo-Fenton degradation experiments were carried out using methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (RhB), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), and bisphenol A (BPA) as organic pollutants (Fig. 12 (a)). All the pollutants are degraded after 50 min of reaction (Fig. S4). Due to the electronegativity of MO, the electrostatic repulsive force between MO and the catalyst affected the adsorption effect, limiting degradation performance. The π-π interaction between BPA, 2,4-DCP, and CQDs enhances the adsorption effect and improves the degradation efficiency [15].

Furthermore, the mineralization ability of the catalyst for organic pollutants was investigated. Fig. 12(b) shows the TOC removal efficiency for BPA, 2,4-DCP, and phenol. After 20 min of reaction, the TOC removal efficiency is maintained at a low level. This may be due to the formation of intermediate organic products during the mineralization process. Some intermediate products still contain a benzene ring. Only when the benzene ring structure is destroyed by
OH to form organic acids that are more easily decomposed can the TOC removal efficiency be further improved [64]. It was concluded that CQD@FeOOH exhibits general applicability to the decomposition of organic pollutants. The addition of CQDs to the catalyst and the molecular structure and charge characteristic of the organic pollutants affect the degradation.

In order to investigate the photocatalytic activity and structural stability of the catalyst during long-term use, the catalyst 10CQD@FeOOH was evaluated over five repeated degradation experiments. Fig. 13 (a) shows that the catalyst activity does not significantly decrease. The phenol is completely degraded within 60 min in the first four cycles of the test. In the fifth test, the degradation efficiency remains at 97.4%, indicating that the catalyst maintains a high activity over a long period of use. Furthermore, the dissolution of iron ions in the photo-Fenton reaction system was detected by atomic absorption spectra (Fig. 13(a)). The concentration of iron ions in the system remains at the low level of 0.025–0.016 mg/L, which is lower than the EU standard of 2 mg/L, indicating that CQD@FeOOH has high stability against (photo) chemical corrosion, and the effect of homogeneous photo-Fenton can be ignored [10]. The crystallinities of 10CQD@FeOOH before and after five repeated experiments were compared (Fig. 13(b)). Comparing with the XRD spectra before the reaction of 10CQD@FeOOH, after five times of recycling, the characteristic diffraction peaks of the crystal plane (1 1 0, 1 3 0, 0 2 1 and 2 1 1) are weakened and the characteristic peak of 110 crystal plane at 21.22° is broadened which is consistent with the amorphous feature material [65], speculating that some amorphous components cover on the surface of nanorods due to the part of grain size becomes smaller [66]. Moreover, no new peaks appear, indicating that no new phases are formed, and the position of the characteristic peaks do not shift, indicating that the lattice plane spacing do not change. Therefore, the catalytic activity of the material has not decreased significantly.

CQDs were successfully introduced to αFeOOH with a facile, economical, and environmentally friendly method that is suitable for large-scale synthesis. Highly dispersed CQDs were anchored onto αFeOOH by forming stable FeOC bonds to construct a three-dimensional flower-like CQD@FeOOH structure. Under the combined action of light irradiation and H2O2 to form a photo-Fenton system, CQD@FeOOH exhibits excellent ability to degrade phenol. This is because CQDs as photosensitizers and electron acceptors have excellent electron conductivity, which broads the photo-response range of the photocatalytic material αFeOOH, enhances light capture capacity, realizes rapid electron transfer, and accelerates the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. It was found that a low concentration of H2O2 and a wide pH range can provide high catalytic activity, which is essential to reducing operating costs. Furthermore, CQD@FeOOH also exhibited effective degradation efficiency and excellent reliability on various organic contaminants with different surface charges under synergic photo-Fenton system. Therefore, the CQD@FeOOH hybrid materials have a broad application prospect as a highly efficient and facile heterogeneous catalyst in the photo-Fenton treatment of organic wastewater.
